nodes.title: Clone first to keep input unchanged
nodes.why: `sort()` mutates arrays in place, so cloning prevents side effects on the original input.
nodes.tradeoff: Safer function behavior with a small extra memory cost.

nodes.title: Use numeric comparator for correct ordering
nodes.why: Without a comparator, JavaScript sorts values as strings, which breaks numeric order.
nodes.tradeoff: Correct numeric results, with slightly more code.

nodes.title: Switch asc/desc based on the flag
nodes.why: The flag controls sort direction in one comparator, keeping the API simple.
nodes.alternative: Always sort one way and transform afterward.
nodes.tradeoff: Flexible and explicit, but comparator logic is a bit longer.

nodes.title: Always sort ascending first
nodes.why: A single comparator handles numeric ordering clearly and consistently.
nodes.tradeoff: Cleaner comparator, but requires an extra step for descending.

nodes.title: Reverse only when descending is requested
nodes.why: After ascending sort, reversing gives descending order with very little logic.
nodes.alternative: Use `b - a` comparator for descending directly.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple to read, but adds an extra O(n) reverse pass.

nodes.title: Return the sorted copy
nodes.why: Returning the cloned array keeps the function pure from the caller’s perspective.
nodes.alternative: Mutate and return the original array.
nodes.tradeoff: Predictable API behavior, with one array copy overhead.
